US Loan Default Analysis

A Report for the Credit Institute of Texas

Student 26572982

June 18, 2026

1 Executive Summary

This report analyses 1 million peer-to-peer loans from Lending Club to identify key drivers of loan default and evaluate the Credit InstituteвАЩs long-held heuristics about borrower risk. Of the 383,202 resolved loans analysed, 22.5% ended in default вАФ nearly 1 in 4.

Key findings:

  • Credit grade is the strongest and most reliable predictor of default risk
  • DTI above 20 consistently produces above-average default rates вАФ a hard cap of 20 is recommended
  • The InstituteвАЩs heuristics about home ownership and employment length are only weakly supported
  • Texas is unremarkable compared to other US states вАФ no evidence of a distinct default culture
  • Small business loans carry the highest default risk at 33.3% вАФ 1 in 3 loans lost
  • Interest rates are driven by credit grade, not age or occupation

2 Data Overview

Item Detail
Source Lending Club peer-to-peer lending platform (anonymised)
Total loans 1,000,000
Resolved loans analysed 383,202 (Defaulted + Fully Paid)
Overall default rate 22.5%
Loan amounts $1,000 to $40,000
Interest rates 5.31% to 30.99%
States covered All 50 US states

Note on methodology: Analysis was restricted to resolved loans (Defaulted and Fully Paid) only. Current loans (597,668) were excluded as their outcome is unknown. At Risk loans (19,130) were excluded to keep the binary default comparison clean and conservative.

3 Default Rate by Credit Grade

Default rate rises consistently from Grade A (6.9%) to Grade G (57.3%).

Findings:

  • The Lending Club grading system produces a perfect staircase pattern вАФ every grade is worse than the one before without exception
  • Grade A (6.9%) and Grade B (15.6%) are well below the 22.5% average вАФ genuinely safe lending zones
  • Grade C (25.3%) is the inflection point вАФ the first grade to cross above the average
  • Grades E, F, G are extreme risk вАФ more than 1 in 2 Grade F and G loans are lost
  • Within each grade, defaulted and fully paid borrowers received virtually identical interest rates вАФ confirming the grade drives pricing, not outcome

Recommendation: Prioritise Grade A and B loans. Exercise extreme caution with Grade E and above where default rates exceed 43%.

4 Testing the InstituteвАЩs Heuristics

4.1 Heuristic 1: Home Owners Default Less

Mortgage holders default least. Renters default most. Outright owners show no advantage.

Findings:

  • MORTGAGE (18.8%) вАФ below average, the safest category
  • OWN (22.8%) вАФ virtually identical to the overall average, no advantage over renters
  • RENT (27%) вАФ highest default rate, 4.5 points above average

Verdict: Partially supported but misleading. Mortgage holders do default less, but outright home owners offer no protection over renters. The heuristic should be refined: it is mortgage holders, not home owners broadly, who present lower default risk. This likely reflects that mortgage holders have demonstrated creditworthiness to obtain a mortgage in the first place.

4.2 Heuristic 2: 10+ Years Employment Means Lower Default

Employment length shows minimal variation in default rates across all categories.

Findings:

  • 10+ years (20.7%) does show the lowest default rate вАФ but only 1.8 percentage points below average
  • The spread across all employment lengths is only 3 percentage points (20.7% to 23.7%)
  • The pattern is essentially flat вАФ employment length explains very little variation in default risk

Verdict: Not supported as a strong predictor. While 10+ year employees show slightly lower default rates, the effect is too small to be a reliable screening tool. Employment length alone should not be used as a primary risk indicator.

5 DTI Analysis and Hard Cap Recommendation

Default rate rises consistently with DTI. DTI above 20 crosses the average threshold.

What is DTI? The Debt-to-Income ratio measures the percentage of a borrowerвАЩs gross monthly income that goes toward existing debt obligations. A high DTI means the borrower is already heavily committed before taking a new loan, leaving little buffer for unexpected expenses.

Findings:

  • Clear upward trend вАФ every DTI band produces higher defaults than the one below it
  • DTI bands 0-5 through 15-20 all sit below the average вАФ safe lending zones
  • DTI 20 is the inflection point вАФ above this the default rate crosses and stays above average
  • DTI 35+ reaches 32% default rate вАФ nearly 1 in 3 loans lost

DTI Hard Cap Recommendations by Risk Tolerance:

Risk Tolerance Recommended DTI Cap Expected Default Rate
Conservative 20 Below 21%
Moderate 25 Below 25%
Aggressive 30 Below 28%
Avoid Above 30 31-32%

Does DTI cap differ by state? Given that Texas and most states cluster around the national average, a uniform DTI cap of 20 is recommended nationally. State-specific adjustments are not warranted given the narrow spread in state-level default rates.

6 State Analysis: Is Texas Different?

Texas (red bar) sits at the national average. No evidence of a distinct default culture.

Findings:

  • Texas sits approximately in the middle of all US states with a default rate just above the 22.5% average
  • DC has the lowest default rate at around 13% вАФ WashingtonвАЩs high income levels likely explain this
  • Arkansas has the highest default rate at around 27%
  • Most states cluster tightly within a narrow 8-10 percentage point band

Verdict: Texas is not different. The data does not support the notion that US states differ significantly in their default culture. Geographic location is a poor predictor of default risk compared to credit grade and DTI, which show far stronger patterns.

7 Interest Rate Analysis

Interest rates rise consistently by grade. Defaulted and fully paid borrowers received virtually identical rates within each grade.

Findings:

  • Interest rates rise consistently and clearly from Grade A (~7-8%) to Grade G (~29-30%)
  • Within each grade, the red (Defaulted) and green (Fully Paid) boxes overlap almost perfectly вАФ meaning Lending Club cannot distinguish future defaulters from payers at the time of lending
  • Interest rates are not determined by age or occupation as the Institute believed вАФ grade is the primary driver

Verdict: The InstituteвАЩs heuristic that interest rates are determined by age and occupation is not supported. Credit grade is the overwhelmingly dominant factor in interest rate determination. This is actually evidence of a well-functioning pricing system вАФ higher risk borrowers pay more.

8 Default Rate by Loan Purpose

Small business loans carry the highest default risk. Car loans are the safest.

Findings:

  • Small business (33.3%) вАФ highest risk. Business loans are speculative by nature вАФ unlike asset-backed loans, their repayment depends entirely on future business success which is uncertain
  • Car loans (16.6%) вАФ lowest risk. Backed by a physical asset that can be repossessed; borrowers also tend to be more financially stable
  • Debt consolidation (23.8%, n=218,017) вАФ above average and the most common loan purpose. Borrowers consolidating debt are already financially stressed вАФ taking a new loan to pay old loans is a warning sign
  • Credit card and home improvement loans sit below average вАФ associated with more financially stable borrowers

Recommendation: Apply stricter screening to small business and debt consolidation loans. These two categories together represent significant portfolio risk.

9 Summary: Are the InstituteвАЩs Heuristics Realistic?

Heuristic Verdict Evidence
Home owners default less Partially supported Mortgage holders default less (18.8%) but outright owners show no advantage (22.8%)
10+ year employment = lower default Weakly supported Only 1.8 point difference вАФ not a reliable predictor
Credit grades predict default Strongly confirmed Clear staircase from 6.9% (A) to 57.3% (G)
Interest rates determined by age/occupation Not supported Grade is the primary driver of interest rate
States differ significantly in default culture Not supported Most states cluster within a narrow 8-point band

10 Recommendations to the Director

  1. Use credit grade as the primary screening tool вАФ it is the most reliable predictor available, with default rates ranging from 6.9% to 57.3% across grades A to G

  2. Implement a DTI hard cap of 20 for conservative lending вАФ borrowers above this threshold consistently exceed the average default rate

  3. Refine the home ownership heuristic вАФ focus on mortgage holders specifically rather than all home owners. Outright ownership does not reduce default risk

  4. Do not use employment length as a primary screen вАФ the effect is too small to be meaningful. Use it only in combination with grade and DTI

  5. Apply stricter screening to small business loans вАФ at 33.3% default rate, these require additional due diligence or higher interest rate compensation

  6. Treat Texas like any other state вАФ no special policy adjustments are needed for Texas borrowers

  7. Monitor debt consolidation loans closely вАФ at 218,017 loans and 23.8% default rate, this category represents the largest absolute default risk in the portfolio

Data source: Lending Club anonymised loan data. Analysis based on 383,202 resolved loans (Defaulted and Fully Paid) from a total dataset of 1,000,000 loans.
